1: parēsan de tines en autō tō kairō apangellontes autō peri tōn Galilaion — some were present at that very time reporting to him about the Galileans — en autō tō kairō (at that very time — the kairos of Luke 12:56, the opportune time of the Messiah's presence) links this scene to the preceding. The report concerns Galileans whose blood Pilate had mixed with their sacrifices (ta thymata autōn = their sacrificial animals) — a brutal act of political repression during a temple sacrifice, probably in the Passover context. Pilate had history of violent suppression (cf. Josephus, Ant. 18.60-62, the aqueduct riot).
2: kai apokritheis eipen autois, Dokeite hoti hoi Galilaioi houtoi hamartōloi para pantas tous Galilaious egenonto — answering them he said: do you think these Galileans were sinners more than all other Galileans? — the question confronts the popular theological assumption (cf. Job 4:7; John 9:2) that exceptional suffering indicates exceptional sin. The suffix para pantas (more than all, in comparison with all) is the specific form of the retribution-theology assumption: their worse fate implies their worse sin. Jesus rejects this interpretive framework directly.
3: ouchi, legō hymin, all ean mē metanoēte, pantes homoiōs apoleisthe — no, I tell you, but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish — the counter-move is twofold: first, the denial of the sin-suffering equation; second, the redirection of the lesson. Metanoeō (to repent, change one's mind and direction — Luke's central call; cf. Luke 5:32; Luke 15:7,10; Luke 16:30; Luke 17:3,4; Luke 24:47) is the universal requirement. Pantes homoiōs apoleisthe (you will all likewise perish) — the "likewise" does not predict death by Roman violence for all but perishing in the general eschatological judgment; the specific mode of the Galileans' death illustrates the reality of human vulnerability, not the character of God's judgment.
4: ē ekeinoi hoi dekaokto eph hous epesen ho pyrgos en tō Silōam kai apekteinen autous — or those eighteen on whom the tower in Siloam fell and killed them — the Tower of Siloam is otherwise unattested; the pool of Siloam (south of the City of David) was part of Hezekiah's water-tunnel system. A tower collapse was an accident — no human agency, no political violence. The pairing of the two incidents (Roman political violence, architectural accident) covers both categories of violent death: human malice and random disaster. dokeite hoti autoi opheiletai egenonto para pantas tous anthrōpous tous katoikountas Ierousalēm — do you think they were debtors more than all the people living in Jerusalem?
5: ouchi, legō hymin, all ean mē metanoēte, pantes hōsautōs apoleisthe — no, I tell you, but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish — the exact same warning repeated for emphasis, now applied to the Jerusalem audience. The repetition of the call to repentance (metanoēsēte) grounds both cases in the same conclusion: the lesson from accidental and political deaths is not "they deserved it" but "mortality is universal and the time for repentance is now." The urgency is eschatological: kairos (Luke 12:56) is passing.
6: Elegen de tautēn tēn parabolēn — he told this parable — the parable of the Barren Fig Tree follows the double repent-or-perish warning as its narrative elaboration. sykēn eichen tis en tō ampelōni autou pephuteumenēn — a man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard — the fig tree planted in a vineyard (ampelōn) is an unusual detail; figs were planted in vineyards (Micah 4:4; 1 Kings 4:25) or separately. The vineyard is Israel's consistent OT symbol (Isaiah 5:1-7; Psalms 80:8-16; Jeremiah 2:21; Ezek 15); the fig tree within it represents a particular element of Israel (possibly Jerusalem or its leadership). kai ēlthen zētōn karpon en autē kai ouch heuren — and came seeking fruit on it and found none.
7: eipen de pros ton ampelourgo, Idou tria etē aph hou erchomai zētōn karpon en tē sykē tautē kai ouch heuriskō — he said to the vinedresser: behold, three years I have been coming seeking fruit on this fig tree and finding none — tria etē (three years) has prompted allegorical readings (three years of Jesus's ministry); structurally, three attempts represents a patient but not indefinitely patient owner who has given the tree multiple seasons. ekkoson autēn; hina ti kai tēn gēn katargei — cut it down; why does it even waste the ground? — katargeo (to make inoperative, render useless, waste — Paul's favorite word for the abolition of what has served its purpose) applies to soil exhausted by an unproductive tree; the tree's presence prevents productive use of the land.
8: ho de apokritheis legei autō, Kyrie, aphes autēn kai touto to etos — he answered him: Sir, leave it also this year — the vinedresser's intercession is the parable's pivot: a third party pleads for continued opportunity. heōs hotou skapsō peri autēn kai balō kopria — until I dig around it and throw manure — skaptō (to dig — to aerate and loosen compacted soil around the roots, allowing moisture and nutrients to penetrate) and kopria (dung, manure — the fertilizer that accelerates growth) are the two interventions the vinedresser will provide. The intensification of care before the final deadline: the tree will be given every possible advantage before the verdict of fruitlessness becomes final.
9: kan men poiēsē karpon eis to mellon — if it then bears fruit in the coming year — the conditional is hopeful but not guaranteed; the vinedresser pleads for a chance, not a certainty. ei de mē ge, ekkopseis autēn — but if not, you will cut it down — the deadline is deferred, not abolished; the owner's patience accommodates the vinedresser's intercession but does not abandon the fruitfulness requirement. The parable ends without resolution: the audience must supply the ending. In the narrative context, Israel is at the year of deferral — the three years of Jesus's ministry giving way to one final year of intensified opportunity.
10: Ēn de didaskōn en mia tōn synagōgōn en tois sabbasin — he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the Sabbath — the Sabbath healing of the bent woman (Luke 13:10-17) is unique to Luke. The setting — synagogue, Sabbath — is the intersection of maximum communal witness and maximum halakhic scrutiny. The woman's presence in the synagogue despite her severe disability (pneumati astheneia, Luke 13:11) implies she maintained her religious practice throughout the eighteen years of her affliction.
11: kai idou gynē ēn pneumati echousa astheneia etē dekaokto — and there was a woman who had a spirit of weakness for eighteen years — pneuma astheneia (spirit of infirmity/weakness) — Luke names a spiritual dimension to the physical condition (Luke 13:16 confirms: "bound by Satan"); this does not eliminate natural causation but adds a demonic oppression diagnosis. Dekaokto etē (eighteen years) — the same number as the tower victims (Luke 13:4), an intentional echo: these eighteen years of bondage parallel those eighteen deaths, both serving to illustrate the condition from which Jesus comes to liberate. kai ēn sygkyptousa kai mē dynamenē anakupsai eis to panteles — she was bent double and could not straighten up at all.
12: idōn de autēn ho Iēsous prosephōnēsen kai eipen autē — seeing her, Jesus called to her and said — the initiative is entirely Jesus's: the woman makes no request, no approach. Prosephōnēsen (called out to, summoned by calling) is a public summons across the synagogue. Gynai, apolelalysai tēs astheneia sou — Woman, you have been released from your weakness — apolelalysai (perfect passive: you have been and remain released — the release is an accomplished fact declared before the laying on of hands) announces freedom before the physical restoration. The perfect tense is the word of liberation: the sentence is pronounced, the bondage broken.
13: kai epethēken autē tas cheiras — and he laid his hands on her — the laying on of hands (epitithēmi tas cheiras) is the gesture of blessing and transfer: the same gesture used for healing (Luke 4:40), commissioning (Acts 6:6; Luke 13:3), and gift of the Spirit (Acts 8:17-19). kai parachrēma anōrthōthē kai edoxazen ton Theon — and immediately she was made straight and was glorifying God — anorthoō (to straighten up, erect again — medical term for correcting a bent or collapsed structure; cf. Acts 15:16 "raise up the tent of David that has fallen") is the physical restoration. Parachrēma (immediately) is Luke's healing-stamp. Edoxazen ton Theon (was glorifying God) — the imperfect of continuous praise: she did not stop.
14: apokritheis de ho archisyagōgos, aganaktōn hoti tō sabbatō etherapeusen ho Iēsous — the synagogue ruler answered, indignant because Jesus had healed on the Sabbath — aganakteō (to be indignant, greatly displeased — a strong word for righteous anger turned against Jesus) frames the synagogue ruler's objection as a principled defense of Sabbath law. eischen tō ochlō, Hex hēmerai eisin en hais dei ergazesthai; en autais oun erchomenoi therapeuesthe kai mē tē hēmera tou sabbatou — he said to the crowd: there are six days on which it is necessary to work; in those come and be healed and not on the Sabbath day — the reproof is addressed to the crowd (not directly to Jesus), probably to avoid direct confrontation; the halakhic point is technically sound within a strict Shammaite framework.
15: apekrithē de autō ho Kyrios kai eipen, Hypokrita, hekastos hymōn tō sabbatō ou lyei ton boun autou ē ton onon apo tēs phatnēs kai apagagōn potizei — the Lord answered him and said: Hypocrites! Does not each of you on the Sabbath untie his ox or donkey from the manger and lead it away to water? — Hypokritai (Hypocrites — the plural indicts the synagogue leadership in general, not just the ruler). The argument is qal va-homer (light to heavy, a fortiori): if one may perform the labor of untying and leading animals on the Sabbath for their welfare (a permissive halakhic ruling accepted by most schools, m. Eruvin Luke 2:1-4), how much more may one release a human being from bondage.
16: tautēn de, thygatera Abraam ousan, hēn edēsen ho Satanas idou dekaokta etē — and this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan has bound for eighteen years — thygatera Abraam (daughter of Abraham) restores the woman's full covenant identity: she is not defined by her disability or her gender's exclusion from the inner synagogue space but by her Abrahamic inheritance. Hon edēsen ho Satanas (whom Satan has bound — aorist: a specific binding at a point in time, followed by eighteen years of captivity) names the spiritual agency behind the physical condition. ouk edei lythēnai apo tou desmou toutou tē hēmera tou sabbatou — ought she not be loosed from this bond on the Sabbath day? — dei (it is necessary, it is morally required) inverts the ruler's "six working days" argument.
17: kai tauta legontos autou katēschynonto pantes hoi antikeimenoi autō — and as he was saying these things all his opponents were put to shame — kataischynō (to put to shame, dishonor publicly — cf. Isaiah 41:11; 54:4 LXX) is the reversal of honor: those who claimed halakhic superiority are publicly shamed. kai pas ho ochlos echaire epi pasin tois endoxois tois ginomenois hyp autou — and all the crowd was rejoicing at all the glorious things being done by him — endoxois (glorious, magnificent things) is Luke's word for miraculous events that display divine glory (cf. Luke 7:25; Acts 10:46). The crowd's joy is the positive counterpart to the opponents' shame.
18: Elegen oun, Tini homoia estin hē basileia tou Theou kai tini homoiōsō autēn — he said therefore: what is the kingdom of God like? and what shall I compare it to? — homoios/homoioō (like, to liken — the comparison-formula for parables) introduces the two kingdom-parables. The double question (Tini homoia... kai tini homoiōsō) is the rhetorical invitation to thought before the comparison is offered; it solicits the audience's creative engagement. The kingdom-parables follow the Sabbath healing as its interpretation: the liberation of the bent woman is an instance of the kingdom's arrival, now described through the lens of growth and pervasion.
19: homoia estin kokkō sinapeōs, hon labōn anthrōpos ebalen eis kēpon heautou — it is like a mustard seed which a man took and threw into his garden — the mustard (sinapi) seed is proverbially small in rabbinic literature (b. Sanhedrin 91a: "as small as a grain of mustard"). Kēpos (garden — Luke's word; Matthew/Mark have "field") may reflect a Hellenistic context. kai ēyxēsen kai egeneto eis dendron — and it grew and became a tree — Luke's dendron (tree) is a larger growth than Mark's "larger than all garden plants"; Luke's version is more clearly hyperbolic. kai ta peteina tou ouranou kateskēnōsen en tois kladois autou — and the birds of the air nested in its branches — an allusion to Daniel 4:12,21 (Ezekiel 17:23; Luke 31:6) where a great tree shelters the nations: the kingdom's growth will accommodate all peoples.
20: kai palin eipen, Tini homoiōsō tēn basileian tou Theou — again he said: what shall I compare the kingdom of God to? — the second question and second kingdom-parable maintains the paired structure (cf. the lost sheep/lost coin parables in Luke 15; the two sons/prodigal in Luke 15). The repetition of the question (palin = again) marks the separate parable; the yeast-parable supplements the mustard-seed by describing the kingdom's mode of pervasion rather than its mode of growth.
21: homoia estin zymē, hēn labousa gynē enekrypsen eis aleurou sata tria heōs hou ezymōthē holon — it is like yeast which a woman took and hid in three measures of flour until it was all leavened — zymē (yeast, leaven) here is used positively, in contrast to Luke 12:1 (the leaven of hypocrisy). The woman hides (enkryptō — to conceal, bury within) the yeast: the kingdom works invisibly from within, not through external imposition. Sata tria (three seahs — approximately 40 liters, enough flour for a feast for 100+ people; cf. Genesis 18:6 where Sarah prepares three seahs for the divine visitors). The large quantity implies communal, abundant transformation. Heōs hou ezymōthē holon (until the whole was leavened) — complete, total pervasion.
22: kai dieporeueto kata poleis kai kōmas didaskōn kai poreian poioumenos eis Ierousalēm — he was traveling through cities and villages teaching and making his way to Jerusalem — this verse is Luke's narrative hinge reminding the reader that the entire discourse of Luke 12-13 occurs within the travel narrative (Luke 9:51-19:44). Poreian poioumenos eis Ierousalēm (making his journey toward Jerusalem) — poreian poieomai (to make a journey, pursue a course) uses the journey-language that defines the central section of Luke's Gospel. Every teaching occurs under the shadow of the destination.
23: eipen de tis autō, Kyrie, ei oligoi hoi sōzomenoi — someone said to him: Lord, are only a few being saved? — the question was debated in Second Temple Judaism: how many of Israel would be saved in the eschatological age? Some traditions held that "all Israel has a share in the world to come" (m. Sanhedrin Luke 10:1); others expected only a remnant (4 Ezra 8:1-3: "the Most High made this world for the sake of many, but the world to come for the sake of few"). Jesus refuses to answer the numerical question directly and redirects to the urgency of personal decision.
24: ho de eipen pros autous, Agōnizesthe eiselthein dia tēs stenēs thyras — he said to them: strive to enter through the narrow door — agōnizomai (to strive, struggle, compete — the athletic-contest word; from agōn, the Greek athletic competition; used by Paul in 1 Corinthians 9:25; Colossians 1:29; 1 Timothy 6:12) describes effortful, concentrated striving. Stenē thura (narrow door — Matthew's version has "narrow gate" in a different context, Matthew 7:13-14). The narrowness does not mean restriction to a few but difficulty of entry: the door requires focused intention. hoti polloi, legō hymin, zētēsousin eiselthein kai ouk ischysousin — because many, I tell you, will seek to enter and will not be able.
25: aph hou an egerthē ho oikodespotēs kai apokleisē tēn thyran — once the master of the house gets up and shuts the door — the closing of the door is the definitive moment of eschatological division: before its closing, entry is possible; after, impossible. Apokleisē (to shut, lock — to close definitively against reentry) is the aorist of finality. kai arxēsthe exō hestanai kai krouein tēn thyran legontes, Kyrie, anoixon hēmin — and you begin to stand outside and knock at the door saying: Lord, open to us — Kyrie (Lord) is addressed to the one who has shut the door; the address is that of those who assumed relationship but lacked entry. kai apokritheis erei hymin, Ouk oida hymas pothen este — answering he will say: I do not know you, where you are from.
26: tote arxesthe legein, Ephagomen enōpion sou kai epiomen kai en tais plateiais hēmōn edidaxas — then you will begin to say: we ate and drank before you and you taught in our streets — the three claims are social proximity to Jesus: table-fellowship (ephagomen enōpion sou = we ate before/with you), shared drink, and reception of his teaching. None of these constitute the relationship of discipleship; proximity without personal commitment and transformation is not the basis of entry. The claims echo Luke 4:23 ("whatever we heard done in Capernaum, do here also") — familiarity without faith.
27: kai erei legōn hymin, Ouk oida hymas pothen este; apostēte ap emou, pantes ergatai adikias — and he will say, speaking to you: I do not know you, where you are from; depart from me, all workers of iniquity — Ouk oida hymas pothen este (I do not know you, where you are from) repeated for emphasis: the denial of relationship is total. Ergatai adikias (workers of iniquity — a phrase from Psalms 6:8 LXX: "depart from me all workers of iniquity") identifies those who claimed proximity to Jesus but practiced injustice as Psalm 6's evildoers. The citation grounds the saying in the Psalter's lament-tradition.
28: ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn — there will be the weeping and the gnashing of teeth — the stereotyped eschatological expression of grief and rage in final exclusion (Matthew uses it 6x; Luke only here). hotan opsēsthe Abraam kai Isaak kai Iakōb kai pantas tous prophētas en tē basileia tou Theou, hymas de ekballomenous exō — when you will see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God and yourselves thrown out — the excluded see the patriarchs inside the kingdom; the vision of inclusion they assumed for themselves belongs to others while they are ekballomenous exō (thrown outside).
29: kai hēxousin apo anatolōn kai dysmōn kai apo borra kai notou kai anaklithēsontai en tē basileia tou Theou — and they will come from east and west and from north and south and will recline at table in the kingdom of God — apo anatolōn kai dysmōn kai apo borra kai notou (from east and west and from north and south) is the four-direction formula for universal inclusion (cf. Isaiah 49:12; Psalms 107:3; Zechariah 8:7). Anaklithēsontai (will recline at table) — the banquet posture; the kingdom is the eschatological feast where the unexpected guests from the margins of the world have places set for them. The reversal operates at the macro scale: those who thought themselves insiders will watch outsiders dine.
30: kai idou eisin eschatoi hoi esontai prōtoi kai eisin prōtoi hoi esontai eschatoi — and behold, there are last who will be first and first who will be last — the reversal aphorism (cf. Luke 18:14; Matthew 20:16; Mark 10:31) summarizes the kingdom's counter-intuitive economy. Eschatoi/prōtoi (last/first) applies both to the unexpected Gentile guests reclining in the kingdom (Luke 13:29) and to the presumptuous insiders excluded (Luke 13:28). The reversal is not guaranteed for everyone outside or denied to everyone inside; it is the description of the kingdom's surprise: ethnic and religious advantage does not convert to eschatological position without the faith-response.
31: En autē tē hōra prosēlthon tines Pharisaioi legontes autō, Exelthe kai poreuou enteuthen, hoti Hērōdēs thelei se apokteinai — at that very hour some Pharisees came saying to him: get out and go from here, because Herod wants to kill you — a surprising warning from Pharisees who are typically hostile (though some Pharisees are hospitable in Luke: Luke 7:36; Luke 11:37; Luke 14:1). The warning may be genuine concern, may be a Herodian attempt to drive Jesus from Perea (Herod's territory), or may be both. Herod Antipas had already killed John the Baptist (Luke 3:19-20; Luke 9:9) and had desired to see Jesus (Luke 9:9; Luke 23:8).
32: kai eipen autois, Poreuthentes eipate tē alōpeki tautē — go and say to that fox — alōpēx (fox) is a term of contempt in both Greek and Jewish usage: cunning, destructive to small animals, not the noble lion. Jesus's public characterization of Herod as a fox is political: it names the petty-prince quality of Herod's threats. Idou ekballō daimonia kai iaseis apotelō sēmeron kai aurion kai tē tritē teleioumai — behold, I cast out demons and complete healings today and tomorrow and on the third day I am perfected — teleioumai (I am perfected, completed, brought to my goal — cf. Hebrews 2:10; Luke 5:9; 12:23 for the same "perfection through suffering" language) is a Passion reference: the "third day" is the resurrection day that completes the journey.
33: plēn dei me sēmeron kai aurion kai tē echomenē poreuesthai — but it is necessary for me today and tomorrow and the next day to travel — dei (divine necessity — Luke's strongest compulsion word) appears again: the journey to Jerusalem is not chosen but appointed. hoti ouk endechetai prophētēn apolesthai exō Ierousalēm — because it is not acceptable for a prophet to perish outside Jerusalem — ouk endechetai (it is not acceptable, not possible, not fitting — an ironic statement) frames Jerusalem's murderous history with prophets as the destination for the prophet who must die. The irony is double: Jerusalem is simultaneously the city of the temple (God's presence) and the city that kills his messengers.
34: Ierousalēm, Ierousalēm, hē apokteinousa tous prophētas kai lithobologousa tous apestalmenous pros autēn — Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the one killing the prophets and stoning those sent to her — the double vocative (Ierousalēm, Ierousalēm) is the mourning address, the form of lament (cf. 2 Samuel 18:33 "O Absalom, my son"; Jeremiah 22:29 "O land, land, land"). Hē apokteinousa ... kai lithobologousa (the one killing ... and stoning) — present participles: the city is characterized by its persistent rejection of prophetic address. posakis ēthelēsa episynaxai ta tekna sou hon tropon ornis tēn heautēs nossian hypo tas pterygas — how often I desired to gather your children as a bird her nest under her wings — the maternal-bird image (Deuteronomy 32:11; Psalms 91:4; Ruth 2:12; Isaiah 31:5) of YHWH's protective sheltering; Jesus speaks with divine longing.
35: kai ouk ēthelēsate — and you were not willing — the single counter to the divine desire: human refusal. The perfect freedom of rejection stands against the perfect compassion of the divine longing. idou aphietai hymin ho oikos hymōn — behold, your house is left to you desolate — ho oikos hymōn (your house) is the temple (cf. Jeremiah 12:7; 22:5 LXX "your house is left desolate"); the divine presence departs from the house that rejects its Lord. legō de hymin, ou mē idēte me heōs [hēxei hote] eipēte, Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati Kyriou — I tell you, you will not see me until you say: Blessed is the one coming in the name of the Lord — Psalms 118:26 (LXX), the Hallel Psalm quoted at the triumphal entry (Luke 19:38). The suspension of the divine presence ends when Jerusalem greets its King; the verse is simultaneously a judgment and a promise.
